coreThesis: Meaningful scale in urban autonomous passenger miles arrives in steps, not a single release. Personal-auto risk pools, liability allocation, and utilization economics adjust with a lag to technology. Over a five-year horizon, that lag creates skew: enablers of fleet-deployed autonomy and compute-intensive validation compound; legacy personal-auto exposure and slow-to-pivot OEM narratives face friction.

drivers.title: Urban AV miles and trip share
drivers.detail: Permitted robotaxi and ride-hail miles, and share of trips in target metros served by supervised or driverless fleets—timing and slope matter more than headlines.

drivers.title: Collision frequency and severity
drivers.detail: Per-mile outcomes where ADS data are comparable to human baselines; insurer loss-cost commentary as a coarse check on whether the pool is repricing.

drivers.title: Regulatory path for fleets
drivers.detail: Domain expansion, incident response, and clarity from permit to commercial service—often the binding constraint on scale.

drivers.title: Liability and indemnification
drivers.detail: How courts, policies, and OEM/fleet contracts assign accident cost as the driver’s role in the stack shrinks.

drivers.title: Utilization and residuals
drivers.detail: Fleet miles per asset, revenue per mile, and passenger-vehicle residual curves as miles migrate toward on-demand supply.
